text: I'd think of this more as a meta-programming - I'm more familiar with max/msp but I think the idea is the same - instead of patching things together (writing the program), you write a program that does the patching for you. I used this technique in max/msp few times to simplify complex patching, and doing this live is obvious next step, but I'm not sure what it can bring that can't be really done without it - for example instead of patching/unpatching a connection from signal to delay, you can just put a mixer in between the and change the volume from 0.0 to 1.0 if that makes sense :wink:

text: Yeah but I'd argue a neo4j is waaaaaay different than max/msp / reaktor even though both have edges and vertices :stuck_out_tongue:
Yeah but I'd argue a neo4j is waaaaaay different than max/msp / reaktor even though both have edges and vertices :stuck_out_tongue:
you want to match nodes, node attributes and relationshipts, that's the same, then with the match you want to do some transformations, that part is not covered in those query languages

> so more like programming the “weights” of the graph…?
hm not sure I understand :slightly_smiling_face:

what I mean is, you can do:
- `sinewave signal -> switch on/off -> output` in reaktor / max/msp directly, and control that switch
- `sinewave signal -> output` and remove/re-add that `->` connection programatically

both have the same output though

A topic near my heart Jack.  Corresponds to meta-programming, parametric modeling, and anything with a rich notion of templating.  Graph theory is less relevant than Applied Category Theory.  At last there's good introductory material <http://www.azimuthproject.org/azimuth/show/Applied+Category+Theory+Course>.

text: I've always liked Rebol (haven't looked into Red quite as much).. I enjoy the language uniformity, the lightweight blocks in particular, and the relatively opinionated "batteries included" is what makes a language practical to me.
That said with the way type systems have advanced, I personally don't want to use dynamically typed languages for anything serious anymore

thanks, I'm definitely going to watch the video today! I agree about dynamic typing—just have a vague sense that 1st-cladd environments might be able to get me staged computation/really sophisticated-but-easy-to-understand types for free by using them as macros/DSLs (not sure how to articulate)—rebol's concept of dialects seems somewhere along that path
Yes, don't quite see the connection :disappointed: and example may help
